nativeRange.habitat: Slow- to moderate-flowing freshwater streams, rivers and stagnant ponds in central, western and eastern Madagascar; an introduced population is also reported from Mauritius

decorPreferences: open background placement as a solitary specimen

summary: An iconic Madagascan bulb plant prized for its skeletal, lace-like perforated leaves; generally considered one of the more demanding Aponogeton species and best suited to experienced aquarists with stable, nutrient-rich tanks.
careNotes: Aponogeton madagascariensis is famous for its lattice-leaf morphology — the mature leaves are naturally perforated between the veins, giving them a skeletal, fenestrated appearance. This is normal anatomy, not damage or disease, and it develops as the leaves mature. The plant grows from a tuberous rhizome (bulb) that should only be half-buried in the substrate; fully burying the bulb commonly promotes rot, while leaving the sprout tip above the substrate gives the best results. Sources broadly agree it is more difficult than other Aponogeton species: Tropica rates it medium difficulty, Buce Plant calls it moderate-to-advanced, and dedicated care guides such as Shrimp and Snail Breeder describe it as outright difficult — the consensus 'advanced' rating reflects its sensitivity to unstable water parameters and its appetite for clean, well-fertilised conditions. Lighting tolerance varies between sources (Tropica medium, Buce Plant moderate, aqua-fish and Fishkeeping World medium-to-high); medium-to-high is generally advised, with 8–10 hours per day. CO2 is helpful but not strictly required: Tropica lists CO2 demand as low, while Buce Plant and aquarium-care guides recommend supplementation; 'recommended' is used here to reflect the dominant hobbyist advice. Like other bulb-type aponogetons, this species has a natural dormancy cycle: leaves may die back periodically (often after several months of vigorous growth), and the plant may sit dormant for weeks before resprouting. Do NOT discard the bulb during dormancy — keep it in the substrate (or store it cool and slightly damp if removed) and new growth will return. Ethical sourcing note: wild collection of this species for the aquarium trade has historically pushed populations close to local extinction in Madagascar, and most authorities now consider field collection inappropriate even though the formal IUCN listing is Least Concern. Prefer nursery-propagated, tissue-cultured or hobbyist-grown bulbs from reputable suppliers (Tropica, Buce Plant, Florida Aquatic Nurseries, etc.) rather than wild-caught stock. Cross-source disagreement: Wikipedia cites adult leaf length up to 65 cm and Tropica notes 25–50 cm; the value used here (25–60 cm) averages the practical aquarium range from hobby sources. Tank size recommendations vary widely (30 US gal / 114 L floor from Shrimp and Snail Breeder, larger 'specialised aquarium' wording from Tropica); 120 L is used as a rounded conservative minimum given the plant's leaf span and substrate-depth needs.
breedingNotes: Propagated in the home aquarium primarily by separating daughter bulbs / side shoots once they have developed their own roots and several leaves. Mature plants can be propagated by splitting the rhizome with a clean blade, dusting cut surfaces with activated carbon, and replanting both halves. Seed propagation is occasionally possible when an emersed flower spike (up to ~1 m tall) reaches the water surface and is hand-pollinated, but seed propagation is rare in home setups. Annual bulb inspection — lifting the plant, trimming away any soft/rotten tissue, and replanting half-buried — is widely recommended.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied search URL resolved to this product page. Used for type (bulb/onion), lighting demand (medium), CO2 demand (low), growth rate (medium), placement (solitary, large aquaria), substrate (half-buried bulb), height (25–50 cm), origin (Africa/Madagascar) and difficulty (medium).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied secondary. Used for native range (Madagascar), bulb planting depth (half-buried, do not fully bury), and dormancy behaviour (perennial; most leaves die back, typically in summer, with new growth in autumn).

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Aponogetonaceae), pH range (6–7.5), care level (moderate-to-advanced), CO2 (recommended) and substrate (nutrient-dense aquarium soil). Care-level rating averaged with other sources to 'advanced'.

additional.name: Shrimp and Snail Breeder (aquariumbreeder.com)
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (20–24 °C), pH (5.5–7.5), gH (2–10), adult size (up to ~60 cm), minimum tank (~120 L / 30 US gal), propagation (daughter bulbs and seeds), substrate depth (5–7 cm), dormancy and bulb-inspection guidance, and 'difficult' rating.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Aponogeton madagascariensis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Aponogetonaceae, order Alismatales), synonyms (Aponogeton fenestralis), native range (Madagascar + Comoros), leaf morphology (oblong, 15–55 cm × 5–16 cm, total leaf up to 65 cm, lattice-of-veins anatomy) and conservation history. Wikipedia reports the formal IUCN listing as 'Least Concern' (3.1); task brief flags 'Vulnerable' — used the verified IUCN listing here but documented the ethical-sourcing concern in careNotes regardless, since aquarium-trade overcollection has historically threatened wild populations.
